title: GUIDELINES ON INTEGRATED MONITORING AND SURVEILLANCE   OF

sections.section: GUIDELINES ON INTEGRATED MONITORING AND SURVEILLANCE   OF
sections.summary: Document title heading for the guidelines on integrated monitoring and surveillance (non‑substantive).
Standard identifier heading: 'FOODBORNE ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE CXG 94-2021' (non‑substantive).
Administrative note stating the guideline was adopted in 2021.

Scope: guidelines cover design and implementation of integrated monitoring and surveillance programmes for foodborne AMR and AMU across the food chain and food production environment, may be informed by human AMR/AMU data though not covering human programmes, cover foodborne pathogens and indicator bacteria, and exclude antimicrobials used as biocides/disinfectants.
States that definitions from CXG 77-2011 and CXC 61-2005 apply and provides additional specific definitions to establish common understanding for the guidelines.
Defines 'antimicrobial agent' as any natural, semi‑synthetic or synthetic substance that at in vivo concentrations kills or inhibits microorganism growth by interacting with a specific target.

Defines 'antimicrobial resistance (AMR)' as the ability of a microorganism to multiply or persist at increased levels of an antimicrobial agent relative to a susceptible counterpart of the same species.
Defines 'food chain' as the production‑to‑consumption continuum including primary production, harvest/slaughter, packing, processing, storage, transport, retail distribution and consumption.
Defines 'foodborne pathogen' as a pathogen present in food that may cause human disease or illness through consumption of contaminated food or products produced by the pathogen.
Defines 'food production environment' as the immediate vicinity of the food chain where evidence indicates it could contribute to foodborne AMR.
Defines 'hazard' for these guidelines as antimicrobial‑resistant microorganism(s) and/or resistance determinant(s).

Defines 'One Health approach' as a collaborative, multisectoral, transdisciplinary approach recognizing interconnections between humans, animals, plants and their shared environment to achieve optimal health outcomes.
Defines 'Plants/Crops' as plants or crops cultivated or harvested as food or feed.

Principles for programme design: apply a One Health approach; integrate monitoring and surveillance as part of national AMR strategies; use risk analysis to guide design, implementation and evaluation; generate AMR/AMU data across relevant sectors; tailor programmes to national priorities and enable continuous improvement; prioritize the most relevant foodborne AMR issues; detect emerging trends and support epidemiological investigation; ensure laboratory quality assurance/management; and harmonize methods, data collection and reporting to improve comparability and multisectoral data exchange.

Risk‑based approach: a risk analysis framework (CXG 77-2011) should inform the development, implementation and evaluation of monitoring and surveillance programmes, programme data should inform risk assessment and control measure decisions, and where domestic AMR data are limited programmes may start based on available scientific knowledge and other information sources.

Regulatory framework and roles: competent authorities should provide good governance and collaborate across human, animal, plant/crop, environment and other sectors, involve relevant stakeholders, encourage voluntary international data sharing, and ensure access to all available national sources of relevant data.

sections.section: 7. PRELIMINARY  ACTIVITIES  FOR  THE  IMPLEMENTATION  OF  AN  INTEGRATED  MONITORING  AND SURVEILLANCE PROGRAMME(S) FOR FOODBORNE AMR
sections.summary: Preliminary activities: implementation of monitoring and surveillance should include pilot studies and testing to inform programme design, with countries progressing and continuously improving activities according to national objectives, infrastructure, capabilities, resources and new scientific knowledge.

sections.section: 11. KEY  DESIGN  ELEMENTS  TO  BE  ESTABLISHED  BEFORE  INITIATING  THE  MONITORING  AND SURVEILLANCE ACTIVITIES
sections.summary: Key design elements before initiating monitoring: for AMR, identify highest‑priority microorganisms, antimicrobial panels and sample sources, sampling points and frequency, representative sampling plans, laboratory analysis and reporting protocols, and standardized/harmonized methodologies; for AMU, map antimicrobial distribution chains, identify data collection points and stakeholders, assess need for legal frameworks for sales/use data, allow voluntary data collection with stakeholder agreement, and consider relevant OIE guidance.

Specifies required contents for an AMR sampling plan including the sample collection procedure, sample size with statistical methods and assumptions (representativeness, expected prevalence, population size), statistical power/precision/objectives, and strengths/limitations affecting interpretation, and lists considerations to guide design (active vs passive strategy; target animal/plant species, commodities and points in the food chain; strata/risk clusters; opportunities for metadata; target microorganisms/resistance determinants; sampling frequency; known prevalence/seasonality; and SOPs for who collects samples, traceability/biosecurity/quality assurance, and storage/transport), allowing initial limited implementation and gradual expansion of sample sources as resources permit.

Guides selection of sample sources for foodborne AMR surveillance to reflect relevant direct and indirect exposure pathways, national production and consumption patterns and species prevalence, and recommends integrated sampling across relevant points of the food chain with representative samples from food-producing animals (farm and slaughter), food (processing to retail), plants/crops (pre- and post-harvest) and the food production environment, while specifying target microorganisms (foodborne pathogens and indicator bacteria), resistance determinants, and staged testing/characterization (phenotypic AST, possible genetic testing and species-level/molecular analysis as feasible).

Requires laboratories in AMR monitoring/surveillance to perform bacterial isolation, species/serotype identification, typing and AST using standardized validated methods by trained personnel; maintain quality assurance/management systems or accreditation; participate in external proficiency testing; preserve sample integrity with appropriate storage/records; store isolates and reference strains to maintain viability and purity; and have access to national or international reference laboratories for technical support and molecular characterization.
States that antimicrobial susceptibility testing should use methods standardized and validated by nationally or internationally recognized organizations where available.

Requires inclusion of quality control strains and consistent interpretation of MICs or disk diffusion per EUCAST or CLSI (or programme-specific criteria if unavailable) with quantitative results reported; recommends reporting by ECOFFs (wild type/non-wild type) or clinical breakpoints as used, and mandates retention of raw quantitative data to ensure comparability, detect emerging resistance and support temporal analyses and quantitative risk assessment.

sections.section: 17. THE PANEL OF ANTIMICROBIALS FOR SUSCEPTIBILITY TESTING
sections.summary: Mandates harmonization of the phenotypic antimicrobial panel within national surveillance to ensure continuity and comparability (using consistent class representatives across sources, regions and time), selecting antimicrobials based on target bacteria, clinical/epidemiological relevance and ability to track resistance patterns, and considering use in animal/plant production, cross-resistance profiling and co-selection, with prioritization informed by human-health priority, national context and selection pressures.

sections.section: 21. COMPONENTS OF INTEGRATED MONITORING AND SURVEILLANCE PROGRAMME(S) FOR AMU
sections.summary: Defines 'antimicrobial use (AMU)' to include antimicrobials related to sales, prescriptions/orders, manufacturing, imports/exports and actual administration/application for food-producing animals or plants/crops and warns that sales data reflect volume distributed rather than volume actually administered; provides an enabling framework for countries to establish AMU monitoring appropriate to national resources and directs consideration of OIE Terrestrial and Aquatic Animal Health Codes.

sections.section: 22. DESIGN OF AN INTEGRATED MONITORING AND SURVEILLANCE PROGRAMME(S) FOR ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS INTENDED FOR USE IN FOOD-PRODUCING ANIMALS OR PLANTS/CROPS
sections.summary: Advises countries on designing integrated AMU monitoring by choosing sales and/or use data aligned with objectives, acknowledging limitations of each, encouraging collection of farm-level use data where feasible, selecting appropriate units and indicators, and specifying key elements for data collection: scope of agents/classes, protocols for qualitative/quantitative data, harmonized nomenclature, identification of species/production types, required level of detail (production type, route, reason), dosing information, and technical units for reporting.

Lists AMU data sources—sales data from registration authorities, marketing authorization holders, wholesalers, veterinarians, retailers, feed mills, industry groups etc.; import data from registration/customs authorities (with caution to avoid double counting and account for imports not intended for domestic use); and use data from farm/plant records, company records, prescriptions or surveys—and notes differences between quantities sold and actually used due to loss, expiry, stockpiling or population fluctuations.

Outlines collection and reporting principles for AMU data, treating the numerator as either qualitative descriptors (classes) or quantitative amounts sold/used (requiring attention to units, product identification, package counts and strength), defines the denominator as the exposed animal population or plant/crop area (with additional population characteristics as needed), and notes that multiple units or indicators may be appropriate for reporting depending on national objectives.

Explains collection of AMU data where the numerator may be qualitative (antimicrobial classes) or quantitative (amounts sold/used) and should capture product/agent identification, package counts and strength per unit, while the denominator—when used—should reflect the total food-producing animal population or plant/crop area/quantity exposed and may include species, production type, number, body weight and age to contextualize analysis.
States that multiple units of measurement and/or indicators for reporting sales and/or use data may be appropriate depending on national circumstances and surveillance objectives.

sections.section: 25. INTEGRATED ANALYSIS AND REPORTING OF RESULTS
sections.summary: Recommends structured, centralized or coordinated databases for integrated monitoring to allow easy data extraction and expansion, implementation of confidentiality and data management policies with ongoing validation, and recording of sampling design details (stratification/randomization) to link data across surveillance components.

Advises that data management should use structured, centralized or coordinated databases, include confidentiality and data integrity protections, implement regular data validation, and document sampling designs and plans (including stratification and randomization) to link monitoring components.
